• メンテナンス： 正直、毎回油を差すような丁寧な手入れはしておらず、かなり雑に扱っています。

ヒゲトリマー歴9年。
初代はフィリップスの充電式で3年半。
二代目はブラウンの充電式で5年半。
その経験上、今回は電池式にしました。

充電式のデメリット
①使用したい時に充電不足だとモーターの回転力が弱いので
　ヒゲを巻き込んだり均一にカットされなかったりする。
②かといって充電器に繋ぎっ放しにするとバッテリーの衰弱が早まる。
③使いたい時に充電が弱い時、充電器に繋いで満充電した頃には
　やりたい事の優先順位が変わりトリムする気が無くなっている。
④充電器を水回りに置いておくと水がかかった時が怖いので
　普段は閉まっておいて充電する時に引っ張り出す
　という事をしていたのでいちいち面倒くさい。
⑤2～3年するとバッテリー持ちが悪くなってくるので
　トリムしてる途中段階で切れ味が激減する事に度々イライラしていた。

今回の電池式ヒゲトリマーのメリット
①当たり前だけど電池を入れた瞬間からフルパワーの快適ヒゲトリム生活。
②充電式の様に充電器を必要としないので収納もスッキリ
　これはミニマリストの方には嬉しいと思う。
③以前使用していた充電式ヒゲトリマーよりも軽い、これは嬉しい驚きだった。
④電池式の場合、そもそも充電機構が無い分 値段が安い。

最期に今回の製品のデメリットを言うとすればミリ調整のアタッチメントですね

ブラウンのトリマー を５年くらい使っていたのですが、バッテリーがお亡くなりになってしまったため買い換えました。ブラウンのトリマー は使い勝手がよく、特にカット長の微調整機能が気に入っていました。ただ、充電器機の取り回しが結構面倒でした。悩んだ末、乾電池を使える本製品を選びました。よいところは軽くて取り回しが楽なことですが、おそらく乾電池で駆動させるために可動部のサイズは極小になっています。一見頼りないのですが、機能的には問題ないように思います。その一方で、コストを下げるための機構的な妥協が感じられます。例えばブラウンのトリマー でできたカット長の微調整はできません。これは人によってはかなり気になるのでははないか思います。また、効率よくカットをするためには少し工夫というか慣れが必要です。例えば、癖っ毛のカットについては、ブラウンの方が明らかに楽でした。いい意味でも悪い意味でも日本らしさが出ている製品かと思います。

I bought this to replace an ion-battery powered Panasonic trimmer that was about 10 years old and was disappointed that it:

1. has a minimum length of 0.3mm so you can't get a super close shave
2. it often misses stray hairs as the guard is too thick
3. the batteries you have to install separately do not last very long and can often die mid-shave.
4. the guard is pointless, as it has trouble trimming on the shortest setting due to its thickness, so I can't imagine anyone with massive uncontrollable facial hair wanting to 'adjust' it for consistency.

As a "throw in the bag, I'm off on holiday and don't want to take a plug" type trimmer it will do the trick, but if you want a consistently good trim, I would not choose this product.

コロナ禍で在宅勤務が続き会社に余り出ないし、外に出る時はマスクで口周りを隠しているし、来客対応なども無くなり、この１年半無精髭を続けています。伸ばし始めて程なく、余りに見苦しい、というか食事の際にひげが邪魔になるほど伸びたんで、行きつけの千円床屋(値上げして1,200円)で、全体をトリムしてくれと頼んだら、ヒゲと頭で2,400円取られました。爾来、頭をとヒゲを交互に３週間程度で当たって貰っていたのですが、ふと思いついてこのバリカンを試して見ました。
私のヒゲは画像(Before)の通りかなり１本づつが勝手な方向に生えているんで、濡らしてとかしても中々サクサクとは行かず、３mmで５分以上やってみましたが、思うほどトリムできません。こりゃ使えん代物か？と諦めかけたんですが、ふと思いつきダメ元でアタッチメントを長さ０にセットしてみたら。。
結果画像(After)の通り、少し短い感じですがきれいにトリムできました。これ使えそうです。
以下推測を含むコメントで恐縮ですが、１）使用前後の注油が必要とのことですが、多分きれいに掃除してシリコンオイルスプレーでも吹いておけば良さそうです、２）電池や本体の耐久性はまだ使用開始したばかりなので不明（パナの安価なシェーバー同様多分大丈夫？)、３）今回伸ばし過ぎたのを最初から３mmで揃えようとしたんで、無理が有ったかも知れない（順繰りに長い設定から揃えて行けば旨く行ったかも）、４）週に１回位好みの長さで揃えるようにすれば良さそう、４）総じて使いやすい、気に入りました。

First and foremost, I’m sorry, Panasonic Beard Trimmer…

I did you wrong, you see we were together for almost three years. We showered together every night and I always got you wet. But did I take care of you and treat you like you were important?

No, after all those years I started to notice you getting a little hairy and a little smelly so I discarded you, like a trifle.

I then got a new beard trimmer and while she said she loved to go in the shower I could barely get her wet before she was completely useless. I began to grow shaggy and unkempt. People started noticing.

In a desperation I thought of how well you had treated me for all those years. I looked and there you were, just as I had left you. I put my hand into you drawers and laughed as it had been a year and there was no way I could still turn you on.

Under my fingers I found your button and you surprisingly sprang to life.  Not knowing how long we had together I dragged you to the shower, getting you wet and to my delight you lasted till I was finished and completely shorn.

You pretty much have me as a customer for life and hope everyone else has similar happy endings.

TJohn

I’m comparing the Remington MB6850 and the Panasonic ER-GB40 because they’re both ~$50 trimmers I tried. Buy the Panasonic, or something else.

STUBBLE TRIMMING EFFECTIVENESS. The Pana 19 levels of adjustment and I can get it to the perfect height for my stubble. The Philips is sort of slow, but it will produce beautiful stubble after about 5 minutes. The Remington offers 11 different levels, but it doesn’t trim them consistently. It leaves my face with a patchwork even after 10 minutes of trim time. Because it takes so much work, it leaves my face irritated.

FEATURES: The Remington wins here because of its vacuum, which works really well! It avoids the beard trim spray of which the Panasonic is certainly guilty.  I found that the vacuum feature works well, but be careful not to tip it. The Remington includes a second trimmer head. The Philips doesn’t include any of that, but it does have a nicer charger cradle.

BATTERY: About the same. I can get about 4 full trims on battery for each, but it seems to be better in the Remington. You’ll need it for that one too since it takes so long to trim.

FIT & FINISH: The Remington does not feel like a premium product. It’s clunky and full of hard plastics that looks terrible. But the vacuum and canister is nice to empty. To be honest, the Pana didn’t immediately strike me as a premium product, either but compared to them Remington you appreciate the lack of fake stainless steel and hard-touch plastics everywhere. The Pana rests nicely in the palm, too, and I’ve come to like the understated nature of it.

This trimmer is actually perhaps not nearly as bad as reviews suggest, it's basically a series of choosing the right 1 trick pony application and bending around it's awkward/not very user friendly limitations/backwards operation well enough to not break it in the process...in order to reap the uh...benefits?

First off, the positives:

1.) The battery life is ridiculous, as in it's the longest lasting on a single charge beard trimmer I've ever owned. Although it's competition is notoriously weak, making this competition sort of sad. I still want this battery for my mobile phone. I've been through three Norelco's that after the first 1.5 years were pretty much pointless to not use without the charger frequently, then just leave it on the charger, wired all the time basically.

2.) It's sharp enough out of the box, but absolutely requires lubrication to stay that way. This will come in the form of a drop or two of the included oil (let's face it, most guys lose this pretty quick/can't be bothered to do it with much regularity me included) and a splash through good old fashioned H2O, water in the common tongue, and suddenly it works well, actually really well considering the next plus.

3.) Again as it's intended usage as a "wet trimmer" it's extremely waterproof, can dunk the whole thing and be fine. In essence this should be thought of as strictly a WET trimmer. Drop the DRY usage from your mind entirely, Panasonic commands us to do this by design. Splash it running under the faucet before you start, rinse a few times while trimming. This causes 89% or more of the trimmings to stick to the guard, also lubricates the blades enough for a surprisingly smooth trim as wet hair trims easier than dry...hence all the soap and water when using a traditional razor, which I do as well for the rest of my face (Personally don't keep a full thick grizzly Adams beard either). Then just rinse it off with most of the trimmings going down the drain, pull the guard off (when in the full down position!) and give it a quick hand scrub/rinse the last time. Once you get used to this sort of mind shifting routine....oddly...this trimmer starts to work...really quite well.

Now, the negatives...which seem mostly design flaws to be "different" over function, not to mention introducing highly annoying learning curves...for a trimmer.

1.) By placing the height dial measurements on the back, somehow reacclimating the adjustments to Australian-toilet-crikey-blimey-flush-goes-the othaway-mate, everything anyone has learned about trimmers goes out the window. Up is down, down is up, the clock runs backwards, etc. Raising and lowering turns are opposite from intuition. You gotta look at the back now to see where you are at...and it takes an annoyingly long time to adjust to this if you have been using anything else. I frequently go between moustache and chin heights to no guide straight trimmer for side burns and mustachio over lip, so I adjust a lot. Making this frustration escalate to the point of wanting to smash it, then set is on fire. (Should have been listed as a positive, other than this critical frequently used piece, it's actually built like a tank) Even after finally getting all this right consistently, I still have occasional words with it, especially after a night of drinking. But's I'll say: it's very accurate and adjusts to the millimeter.

2.) Can't be over stressed. The trimmer guide requires you to Lower It All the Way before removing it OR YOU WILL BREAK it. Again, nobody is quite prepared for this, will think it's stuck at midway or highest and try to force it, thereby destroying it immediately or over short time wearing it down to break. Didn't think I had to read the freakin manual (RTFM) on a beard trimmer so much these days...but I do, otherwise you have to get your head around it prior (by reading this review perhaps) if you want it to last more than a a couple days. Then it might last 15 years.

3.) The charging stand indicator light is Red all week. Red when you first put it in. Red when fully charged. Red. Always and forever as long as the trimmer is in it. A Battle Star Galactica Cylon staring bright and deadly at you in the bathroom, ever vigilant, ever watchful. Amazing cheap efficient LED technology in strips for my computer for 14 bucks with a rainbow of colors that blink and breath at my command...this trimmer could not afford one LED with battery charge integration? Fortunately (and clearly by way of quirky cost saving design?), it won't be in the charger often, if you respect battery life/memory, I've gotten 20-30 trims off it without recharge. Even then it just goes a bit weaker but actually still works. Which out of common courtesy is when I finally place it in the charger overnight. Then it's good for another 25. Seriously, this thing almost rivals expensive Nicad power tool battery tech if treated as such. Can toss it in the bag for trips with confidence pretty easily without needing the charger even.

Now that I've somewhat irreversibly altered my routine to suit this trimmer's design whims, is it fantastic? Not really. Is it actually a very good to great AWAYS WET trimmer when adjust carefully and operated per a design that rudely altered the way I trim and cleanup arguably for the better...well yeah...sorta did. Will I get another superior sensibly designed, better all purpose trimmer when I find it and leave this one in the shower (or in a cabinet for long periods of time) for random shower man-scaping needs? Yes.

The charging light just tells you it's plugged in, not whether the trimmer is actually charging or the trimmer is fully charged.  There is no charge level indicator on the trimmer itself.  The electrodes that interface the trimmer and charging stand are set high up on the sides of the trimmer, just where you'd expect to be holding it.  They stick out sideways like the electrodes on Frankenstein's neck.  Hence the "feel" of the trimmer is not "ergonomic", in spite of the ergonomic look.

On the positive side, the trimmer is very quiet, just a low hum, so you can use it late at night or early in the morning without waking people sleeping nearby.  However, you have to oil the blades after each use, so if you travel, you need to carry trimmer oil in your luggage.  There is no travel case to protect the trimmer itself, or your luggage from accidentally spilled oil.

If you store it the typical narrow drawer on either side of the sink, it takes up too much space in the drawer. I had a pretty unique college career, before I settled on my final career of Engineering I studied both marketing and industrial design. In Marketing I learned that a good looking product can be sold and that even something that doesn't work well can be made to be perceived to work well just like the emperors new clothes.

I can only think that the reviews of this product that give if 5 stars are suffering from this mild form of hysteria.

Why? In industrial design I learned that form can follow function or function can follow form. I come from the former school, when I spend a given amount of time trimming my beard using a trimmer in different directions across every area of hair on my face I expect the hair on my face then to be all the same length when I'm done.

Clearly the person who designed this trimmer had absolutely no concern for how it works, as I am sitting here in work after paying the better part of $50 for a trimmer that has left clumps of hair of varying lengths throughout my beard even after I spent an excessive amount of time both last night and this morning attempting to use the trimmer.

My skin is actually sore from pushing down to try to get my beard hair to contact the blade through the guard.

The trimmer looks good, but its guard pushes hair down and away from the blade flattening it and keeping it from being cut. Compare this to a standard hair clippers used by a barber, the guard has no flat parts and acts like a comb pushing through the hair against the way it lies and exposing the hair evenly to the blade.

My wife threw my hair clippers away, she thought this trimmer was so "pretty" that she even threw the box and the receipt away and refuses to return it. You can rest assured however its coming with me the next time I go to the outside garbage.

I am for the moment stuck looking like a vagrant at my job because Panasonic didn't bother to see if their trimmer actually trimmed beards.

So if you want to look cool on Instagram when "trimming" your beard and you want something that looks impressive on the shelf definitely buy this thing.

If you want something that actually cuts hair I would strongly recommend a standard hair clipper like what you find at the barber shop, because it actually works. Cutting my beard with a hair trimmer takes less than ten minutes and its cut evenly and neatly.

I've spent over an hour last night and this morning trying to make this garbage thing meet its stated purpose and I have pieces of hair hanging into my mouth from my mustache, and I have long scraggly patches all over my beard.

Shame on you Panasonic for releasing this product, I will never buy anything from you again.

I bought this trimmer over a year ago  and am in the market for a new one.  Overall it is a very functional beard trimmer, but there are 2 main things that have me looking for a new(er) unit.  I keep my facial hair trimmed (beard & mustache, clean neck) and use this unit about once a week to do that.  It trims well and is easy to clean.  I've never used it in the shower though it is designed for that as well.  Here are the 2 things that I have issue with on my unit:

1.  When the trimmer is set to the max (10) the guard is VERY difficult to remove, and pretty much impossible to reinstall.  It has 2 tabs that must fit in to slots on either side and at that max setting, it's a real bear to do.  Sure, I just roll it down to the 5 or 6 setting, and it's easy.  But if I forget and start to remove it, one side comes out easy while the other gets jammed, and now you just have to tug it and hope you don't break it.

2.  Mine takes a VERY long time to fully charge.  I would estimate it at 30 hours or more.  I don't have to recharge often;  maybe once every 6 weeks I would guess.  So battery life is excellent.  But that one time I needed to trim up before going out with my wife, and I had not trimmed in more than a week... and the battery died.  I thought "no biggie, it should get enough juice in 1/2 hour.."  NOPE.  So now I drop it back on the charger every month to "top it off" and make sure that doesn't happen again.  I do not keep it on the charger since I don't want to "overcook" the battery and shorten the life of it, but maybe I should.

Overall, I'm satisfied with the unit.  it does the job I need it to, and so long as I watch out for those two issues above, I've had no real problems.  So why am I looking to replace it?  Because those 2 "gotchas" really annoy me.  My last trimmer didn't have as long of a battery life, but if I was "caught out", 20 min on the charger gave more than enough to get cleaned up.  it also had changeable guards that popped on & off like a cover.  It comes down to preferences, and having tried this unit and other, I prefer the removable guards.

One last thing... the charging cradle is odd to me.  With the way the trimmer gets inserted it just seems... backwards... a bit awkward... to me.  I travel every 6 weeks on average and prefer a simple charging cord to carry, not this extra bulky cradle thing.  Maybe the designers thought "oooohhhh, we'll make it look all fancy so it's on DISPLAY and guests can OOOHHHH  and AAHHHHHH over the beard trimmer..."  Sorry, I'm not that fancy.  Function wins out for me 90% of the time.  Panasonic has done a very good job, and they will never get everyone to like every aspect of their product.  I don't hate it, but after 18 months of ownership I'm looking for those features that matter more to me.

Meh, it works OK but I'm not too impressed with the layout. Everything seems backwards. Power button and length selection are on the back, which means that you need to constantly turn it around to adjust things and then flip it back over to cut. If you are doing multiple lengths it can be a real pain to have to keep going back and forth.

Even the cradle is backwards and goes against natural ergonomics. If I'm pointing something at my face my hand doesn't have any reason to flip it around just to accommodate the cradle when putting it down. After months of use I'm still putting this in backwards. It's just not a natural movement.

And like most other trimmers, these designers still haven't figured out that putting a reflective surface on the top of the blade will catch any overhead light and make it that much more difficult to navigate your hairline when using two mirrors to trim things yourself. While this has a black head to it, it is made of a shiny material that reflects light and I need to constantly move around to not catch the reflected overhead light.

I'll hang on to it until it dies as it works well enough, but I am not even the least bit thrilled with this trimmer. Flip the power button, length selection and cradle to the other side and this could possibly be worth 4*. It's unnecessarily annoying as is.
